title: Pupil Assessment
subtitle: Recognise normal and abnormal pupil responses.

cards.frontPrompt: What is the job of the pupil?
backPoints: The pupil controls how much light enters the eye.
Bright light makes the pupil smaller.
Darkness makes the pupil larger.
This helps the eye adapt to changing light conditions.

cards.frontPrompt: Press the torch and watch what happens.
backPoints: Light enters the eye.
A signal travels to the brain.
The brain sends a signal back.
The pupil constricts.

cards.frontPrompt: Which cranial nerves are involved in the pupil light reflex?
backPoints: CN II (optic nerve) detects the light.
CN III (oculomotor nerve) carries the response back to the pupil.
Both nerves are needed for a normal light reflex.
cards.memoryLine: CN II in, CN III out.

cards.frontPrompt: How would you describe these pupils?
backPoints: Normal pupils are usually equal.
Normal pupils are round.
Normal pupils react to light.
Always compare both eyes.

cards.frontPrompt: What do you notice about these pupils?
backPoints: Unequal pupils are called anisocoria.
Some people naturally have unequal pupils.
A new change may indicate neurological deterioration.
Always compare with previous assessments.

cards.frontPrompt: Shine the light. What do you notice?
backPoints: A fixed pupil does not react to light.
The left pupil remains unchanged.
The right pupil constricts normally.
A new fixed dilated pupil requires urgent escalation.

cards.frontPrompt: Shine the light. How would you describe the response?
backPoints: A sluggish pupil constricts slowly.
It may also constrict less than expected.
Compare both eyes.
Compare with previous assessments.

cards.frontPrompt: Why are pupils checked during neurological assessment?
backPoints: Pupil changes may be an early sign of neurological deterioration.
They can indicate raised intracranial pressure.
They can indicate brain injury.
They can indicate nerve compression.
Drug effects can also affect pupil size and response.
cards.memoryLine: Changes matter more than one isolated reading.

quiz.question: What is the main function of the pupil?
Which cranial nerve detects light entering the eye?
Which cranial nerve constricts the pupil?
What is the term for unequal pupils?
How would you describe a pupil that does not react to light?
A new fixed dilated pupil should be:
Approximately what size are these pupils?
quiz.feedback: The pupils are approximately 2 mm. A visual estimate within 1 mm is accepted.

quiz.question: Approximately what size are these pupils?
quiz.feedback: The pupils are approximately 5 mm. A visual estimate within 1 mm is accepted.

quiz.question: How would you record these pupils?
Select the size of each pupil.
quiz.feedback: Record each eye separately and compare the two sides.
